text:
Phenolic compounds are one of the dominant classes of biomass burning emission precursors that can undergo rapid aqueous-phase reactions in the atmosphere to form secondary organic aerosol (aqSOA). Herein, the aqueous-phase reactions between three model phenols, including phenol (C6H5OH), guaiacol (2-methoxyphenol) and syringol (2,6-dimethoxyphenol), and the two crucial oxidants, triplet excited state of an aromatic carbonyl (3C*) and the hydroxyl radical (·OH), were investigated in detail using aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS), nanospray desorption electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (nano-DESI MS) and ion chromatography (IC).

We identified a wide variety of oxygenated products, ranging from oligomers of up to six monomer units, as well as functionalaized monomers and oligomers containing carbonyl, carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups, and small organic acid anions (such as formate, acetate, oxalate, and malate). The average atomic O/C ratios of the phenolic aqSOA varied in the range of 0.85 to 1.23, and are comparable to those found in LV-OOA measured in the field. While the overall product profiles were similar for a given parent for both oxidants, the 3C* reactions proceeded more rapidly than the ·OH-induced reactions. At the point of consumption where 50% of the parent has reacted, the 3C* reactions produced more oligomers and hydroxylated products.

Thermal analysis with a thermodenuder showed that the oligomeric content and the oxygenation level of the aqSOA govern their volatility. In addition, processed aerosol showed a strong absorption in the UV–visible range. These results indicate that the aqueous oxidation of phenols contributes to the formation of secondary brown carbon which may play a role in the climate influence of wood burning and wildfire emissions in polluted environments.
